title: Reading Your Body Like a Friend

hook.rwenLine: End of the unit. You've practised interoception, the body scan, naming nervous-system states, long exhales, listening to tension, HALT, sleep, movement, and co-regulation. Today we put it all together. The body becomes a friend you check in with — not a vehicle, not a problem.
hook.culturalNote: The integration matters more than any single technique. People who can locate themselves in their bodies, name their state, and intervene appropriately have a different baseline than people who can't. This is the actual outcome of Unit 2.

chunks.native: A 60-second sweep: where am I in my body? What state am I in? What does my body need?
chunks.literal: Integration practice from somatic psychology — combining interoception + state-naming + need-identification

chunks.native: Treating the body the way you would treat a friend who told you what it told you — not contempt, not lecturing
chunks.literal: Self-compassion research (Neff, Gilbert) — kinder internal stance produces better outcomes
chunks.phonetic: How would you speak to a friend in this state?

chunks.native: Doing this once a day for two weeks shifts your default relationship to your body — measurably, not theoretically
chunks.literal: Habit research (Lally et al.) — 21+ days to shift a default; sustained shift after 8-12 weeks

pattern.name: Three questions. Once a day. Like a friend.
pattern.explanation: Ask three questions in this order: Where am I in my body right now? (interoception) What state am I in — regulated, activated, shut-down? (state-naming) What does my body actually need? (intervention). Answer kindly. The whole thing takes 60 seconds. Doing this once a day for two weeks is the entire skill outcome of Unit 2.

examples.native: Shoulders tight. Breath shallow. Stomach okay.
Long exhales. Five minutes outside. Eat something.

pairs.left: Where am I in my body?

exercises.question: After answering the three questions — what makes it stick?

options.text: Forcing yourself to act on every answer
Treating your own body the way you'd treat a friend who told you the same thing
Writing it down in a journal every time

exercises.explanation: Kindness as method. Contempt and lecturing don't shift behaviour long-term. Treating your body with the warmth you'd extend to a friend does.

exercises.prompt: What are the three questions of the daily check-in?
correct: Where am I in my body? What state am I in? What does my body need?
Where am I, what state, what need

exercises.sentence: Treat your body the way you'd treat a _____ who told you what it told you.

exercises.question: How long until daily check-in becomes a new baseline?

options.text: About two weeks to feel different; 8-12 weeks to feel like the default

exercises.explanation: Habit research is clear: about three weeks to feel the shift, two-to-three months for it to be your default. The work compounds.

rwenDialogue.intro: Let's do the full three-question check together, right now. Slowly.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Question 1: Where are you in your body right now? Three pieces of specific data.

userChoices.target: Shoulders tense, breath shallow, jaw soft.

userChoices.native: That's a state, not body data.
userChoices.feedback: That's already question 2 — body first. Try: what does your stomach feel like? Your hands? Your shoulders?

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Question 2: What state are you in? Regulated, activated, or shut-down?

userChoices.feedback: Good. Now question 3 — the kindest question.

lines.speaker: rwen
lines.rwenLine: Question 3: If your body were a friend who told you what it just told you — what would you say it needs?

userChoices.target: A five-minute walk outside, water, and a moment to not be doing anything.
userChoices.feedback: Beautiful. That's the entire skill of Unit 2 in 60 seconds. You're done. Now do it tomorrow.

activeRecall.instruction: From memory — the three questions in order.

correct: Where am I in my body?
Where am I in my body right now?
What does my body actually need?

mission.title: Today's mission — and a 14-day commitment
mission.task: Run the three-question check today. Then commit to doing it once a day for fourteen days. Pick a trigger (after the alarm, while the kettle boils, before lunch) so you don't have to remember to remember.
mission.rwenSignoff: End of Unit 2. You can now find yourself in your own body — and you know what to do when you've found yourself.

phase8.scenario: User has finished Unit 2. Rwen runs a brief debrief: what was the most useful lesson? Where did the practice land hardest? What's the one piece they'll keep doing past today?
phase8.rwenRole: Warm, integrative. Not testing — celebrating that they finished the unit.
phase8.successCriteria: User names one lesson they'll keep, one moment they'll use the check-in.
